key_facts: On November 1, 2024, at approximately 12:54 AM, Plaintiff Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi — a commercial truck driver — was lawfully parked on the dirt shoulder off I-580 East in Livermore, CA, resting in a rental Tesla Model Y due to fatigue, when CHP Officers Santiago Meza-Gonzalez (#023295) and Melano (#023054) approached under the pretext of a welfare check. Upon observing a cup in the vehicle, the officers remarked 'I think it's booze' and shifted to a DUI investigation, then opened the passenger door without consent and conducted a warrantless search of the cupholders, center console, and floorboards — finding no evidence of intoxication or illegal activity. Plaintiff was nonetheless handcuffed, placed in a patrol vehicle, and transported for chemical testing; the DA ultimately declined to file any charges. The incident triggered an administrative chemical refusal designation resulting in suspension of Plaintiff's Class A CDL through February 2026, causing over one year of lost wages and diminished earning capacity. Following arrest, a parole violation hold kept Plaintiff incarcerated from approximately November 1 through November 5–6, 2024, during which time he was not brought before a judge for arraignment and was confined with a homicide suspect who menaced him.
procedural_status: Complaint filed pro se on May 27, 2025. Initial Scheduling Order issued October 28, 2025 by Magistrate Judge Chi Soo Kim; summons issued the same date naming CHP, Officer Melano, Officer Meza-Gonzalez, and the State of California, with a service-of-process deadline of January 26, 2026. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6) on December 10, 2025, noticed for hearing on January 16, 2026 at 1:30 PM before District Judge Dena Coggins. Plaintiff filed a pro se opposition on December 22, 2025, conceding dismissal of the State of California, all official-capacity claims, Count III (Monell), punitive damages, and attorneys' fees, while preserving Count I (Fourth Amendment) and Count II (Fourteenth Amendment) against both officers in their individual capacities and requesting leave to amend. The California Attorney General's Office filed a reply on January 5, 2026, locking in all concessions, seeking dismissal of Monell with prejudice, and requesting full dismissal of the remaining counts with prejudice. The matter is currently awaiting the Court's ruling on Counts I and II.
evidence: Class A CDL suspension record (through February 2026)
Empty container recovered during warrantless search
damages: $175,000 – $450,000+, comprising: 1+ year of lost CDL-dependent wages and reduced future earning capacity from CDL suspension; compensation for approximately 5-day unlawful parole hold without judicial review; emotional distress, fear, and stigma from wrongful arrest and dangerous jail conditions (confinement with menacing homicide suspect); and any recoverable costs under applicable civil rights statutes.
risk: Significant. The primary risk is qualified immunity on both remaining counts: under Wesby and Kisela, Plaintiff must identify precedent that 'squarely governs' these specific facts — an officer encountering a visible cup during a late-night welfare check and escalating to DUI investigation — and no such clearly established authority has been cited. Second, the Iqbal/Twombly pleading risk is acute: key facts that could defeat qualified immunity (e.g., that the cup contained no alcohol and was not in plain view, and that officers fabricated observations) appeared for the first time in the Opposition rather than the Complaint, leaving the operative pleading facially thin. Third, Plaintiff's strategic concessions have narrowed the case to individual-capacity claims on Counts I and II only, eliminating Monell as a path to broader liability. Leave to amend will likely be necessary to cure these pleading deficiencies and incorporate the factual allegations needed to overcome qualified immunity at the 12(b)(6) stage.
